source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs
input_row.description: VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. After you've created a flow log, you can view and retrieve its data in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. It is recommended that VPC Flow Logs be enabled for packet "Rejects" for VPCs.
input_row.rationale: VPC Flow Logs provide visibility into network traffic that traverses the VPC and can be used to detect anomalous traffic or gain insights during security workflows. Impact: By default, CloudWatch Logs will store logs indefinitely unless a specific retention period is defined for the log group. When choosing the number of days to retain, keep in mind that the average time it takes for an organization to realize they have been breached is 210 days (at the time of this writing). Since additional time is required to research a breach, a minimum retention policy of 365 days allows for detection and investigation. You may also wish to archive the logs to a cheaper storage service rather than simply deleting them. See the following AWS resource to manage CloudWatch Logs retention periods: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/DeveloperGuide/Settin gLogRetention.html
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if VPC Flow logs are enabled: From Console: 1. Sign into the management console. 2. Select Services, then select VPC. 3. In the left navigation pane, select Your VPCs. 4. Select a VPC. 5. In the right pane, select the Flow Logs tab. 6. Ensure a Log Flow exists that has Active in the Status column. From Command Line: 1. Run the describe-vpcs command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) to list the VPC networks available in the current AWS region: aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> --query Vpcs[].VpcId 2. The command output returns the VpcId of VPCs available in the selected region. 3. Run the describe-flow-logs command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) using the VPC ID to determine if the selected virtual network has the Flow Logs feature enabled: aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter "Name=resource-id,Values=<vpc-id>" • If there are no Flow Logs created for the selected VPC, the command output will return an empty list []. 4. Repeat step 3 for other VPCs in the same region. 5. Change the region by updating --region, and repeat steps 1-4 for each region.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to enable VPC Flow Logs: From Console: 1. Sign into the management console. 2. Select Services, then select VPC. 3. In the left navigation pane, select Your VPCs. 4. Select a VPC. 5. In the right pane, select the Flow Logs tab. 6. If no Flow Log exists, click Create Flow Log. 7. For Filter, select Reject. 8. Enter a Role and Destination Log Group. 9. Click Create Log Flow. 10. Click on CloudWatch Logs Group. Note: Setting the filter to "Reject" will dramatically reduce the accumulation of logging data for this recommendation and provide sufficient information for the purposes of breach detection, research, and remediation. However, during periods of least privilege security group engineering, setting the filter to "All" can be very helpful in discovering existing traffic flows required for the proper operation of an already running environment. From Command Line: 1. Create a policy document, name it role_policy_document.json, and paste the following content: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "test", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } 2. Create another policy document, name it iam_policy.json, and paste the following content: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action":[ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:DescribeLogGroups", "logs:DescribeLogStreams", "logs:PutLogEvents", "logs:GetLogEvents", "logs:FilterLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } 3. Run the following command to create an IAM role: aws iam create-role --role-name <aws-support-iam-role> --assume-role-policy- document file://<file-path>role_policy_document.json 4. Run the following command to create an IAM policy: aws iam create-policy --policy-name <iam-policy-name> --policy-document file://<file-path>iam-policy.json  5. Run the attach-group-policy command, using the IAM policy ARN returned from the previous step to attach the policy to the IAM role: aws iam attach-group-policy --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::<aws-account- id>:policy/<iam-policy-name> --group-name <group-name> • If the command succeeds, no output is returned. 6. Run the describe-vpcs command to get a list of VPCs in the selected region: aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> • The command output should return a list of VPCs in the selected region. 7. Run the create-flow-logs command to create a flow log for a VPC: aws ec2 create-flow-logs --resource-type VPC --resource-ids <vpc-id> -- traffic-type REJECT --log-group-name <log-group-name> --deliver-logs- permission-arn <iam-role-arn>  8. Repeat step 7 for other VPCs in the selected region. 9. Change the region by updating --region, and repeat the remediation procedure for each region. References: 1. CCE-79202-8 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/flow-logs.html
input_row.impact: By default, CloudWatch Logs will store logs indefinitely unless a specific retention period is defined for the log group. When choosing the number of days to retain, keep in mind that the average time it takes for an organization to realize they have been breached is 210 days (at the time of this writing). Since additional time is required to research a breach, a minimum retention policy of 365 days allows for detection and investigation. You may also wish to archive the logs to a cheaper storage service rather than simply deleting them. See the following AWS resource to manage CloudWatch Logs retention periods: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/DeveloperGuide/Settin gLogRetention.html

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control "Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs" is best suited for an automated audit approach. This decision is based on the following criteria:

- **AWS APIs Available:** VPC Flow Logs are standard AWS properties, and their status can be queried using AWS APIs. Specifically, the EC2 and CloudWatch services provide operations to list VPCs and their flow logs.
  
- **boto3 SDK Methods:** The boto3 SDK provides methods such as `describe_vpcs()` and `describe_flow_logs()` to programmatically check the existence and status of flow logs for each VPC.
  
- **AWS CLI Commands:** The AWS CLI commands `aws ec2 describe-vpcs` and `aws ec2 describe-flow-logs` can also be used to audit VPC flow logs automatically.

- **Control Characteristics:** VPC flow logs are a configurable service-level feature, not requiring visual inspection or manual intervention for verification if APIs are available.

- **Audit Instructions:** The audit section explicitly mentions using CLI commands for the verification of this control, supporting an automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_vpc_flow_logs_enabled_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

To automate the audit of this control, the following steps can be implemented:

1. **Enumerate VPCs:** Use boto3's `describe_vpcs()` method to list all VPCs across the specified AWS regions.

2. **Check Flow Logs:** For each VPC, use `describe_flow_logs()` with a filter on `resource-id` set to the VPC ID. This will determine if there is an active log flow.

3. **AWS CLI Alternative:** Alternatively, the AWS CLI command `aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter "Name=resource-id,Values=<vpc-id>"` can be used to verify existence and status of flow logs for each VPC.

4. **Configuration Validation:** Verify that the flow logs exist and the status is 'Active'. Flow logs should be configured to capture 'Reject' traffic for effective monitoring.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:** 
   - **Pass:** All VPCs have at least one associated flow log that is 'Active' and configured correctly.
   - **Fail:** Any VPC without a flow log or with a non-active flow log.

By implementing these steps, organizations can regularly ensure compliance with the CIS Benchmark control regarding VPC flow logs.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. VPC Flow Logs can be efficiently checked using AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI, making manual inspection unnecessary.
- The use of AWS SDK capabilities, such as boto3 methods (`describe_vpcs()` and `describe_flow_logs()`), and AWS CLI commands (`aws ec2 describe-vpcs` and `aws ec2 describe-flow-logs`), is appropriate for this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The boto3 methods and CLI commands mentioned are valid and correctly used to check the existence and status of VPC Flow Logs.
- The manual steps are not applicable as the automated approach is sufficient and complete.
- The program name `aws_vpc_flow_logs_enabled_check` is descriptive and follows the convention, but it could be more specific by including the focus on 'Reject' traffic, e.g., `aws_vpc_flow_logs_rejects_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- The program name could be improved to reflect the specific focus on 'Reject' traffic, which is a key part of the control. Consider renaming to `aws_vpc_flow_logs_rejects_check`.
- The audit could include a check for the log destination (e.g., CloudWatch Logs or S3) to ensure logs are being captured and stored correctly.
- Consider verifying that the flow logs are configured to capture both 'Accept' and 'Reject' traffic, as this can provide more comprehensive monitoring.
- Mention AWS Config as an alternative for continuous compliance checking. AWS Config can be used to create a rule that checks for the presence of VPC Flow Logs.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the technical details are accurate and comprehensive. The use of AWS APIs and automation is appropriate for this control.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Update the program name to `aws_vpc_flow_logs_rejects_check` to better reflect the specific focus of the control.
- Consider adding checks for log destinations and the type of traffic captured (both 'Accept' and 'Reject') for a more thorough audit.
- The overall approach and technical details are sound, so no major changes are needed beyond these improvements.
